coredata.dc:title: Deposition of porous few-layer WSe2 flakes with high density of exposed edge sites
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Weitao
Li, Yongguang
Jin, Long
Chen, Leifeng
Song, Kaixin
Huang, Xiwei
Hong, Xuekun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, remarkably differing to reported well-defined triangular WSe2 flakes, porous WSe2 flakes with large amount of holes were deposited using graphene quantum dots(GQDs) as promoters. By employing this method, triangular or circular thorough holes can be efficiently deposited on basal plane of WSe2 flakes. The size of holes and density of edges vary with layer number of WSe2 basal plane. Highest density of edges emerges on 4-layer WSe2 flake, which is greatly enhanced by 580% compared to those well-defined triangular 4-layer flakes. Finally we propose that the growth of holes is validated by poor wetting between WSe2 and GQDs, while the final shape is determined by crystal symmetry, diffusion and thermal dynamics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: WSe2
Graphene quantum dot
Porous flake

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth of holes on porous flakes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of VT system; (b), temperature ramping curve used in WSe2 growth; Optical image of (c) monolayer WSe2 flakes, (d)few-layer flakes and (e) thick-layer flakes growing using VT; (f), AFM topographic image of monolayer WSe2 flakes; (g), AFM topographic image of a three layer WSe2 flake; (h), line profiles at marked line positions of (f) (bottom panel) and (g) (top panel).

(a)–(c), optical images of porous WSe2 flakes F1-F3; (d)–(f) AFM topographic images of flakes corresponding to F1-F3 in (a)–(c). The height profiles along the dashed lines in (d)–(f) are shown as insets in each image; (g), Raman spectra of F1-F3; (h) calculated density of edges and area ratio of the porous area. Note the orientation of AFM images are slightly shifted compared to the corresponding optical images. In the inset of (h), dashed green lines envelop the flake, black solid lines refer to the edges of flake. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a)–(d) Optical images of porous WSe2 flakes at different growing state. (e)–(h), AFM topographic images of the corresponding flakes in (a)–(d). Note in (e), height profile at the marked line is plotted as inset. A zoomed-in image of circular hole is shown as inset of Fig. 3(f). Note the orientation of AFM images are slightly shifted compared to the corresponding optical images.

Optical images of MoS2 flakes deposited with GQDs as promoters: (a) monolayer; (b) thin-layers; (c) thick-layers. (d)–(f) AFM topographic images corresponding to marked area in (a)–(c); (g) Raman spectra of monolayer and thick-layer flakes deposited with GQDs as promoters; (h), PL spectra monolayer and thick-layer flakes deposited with GQDs as promoters. Note in (h) the PL of monolayer MoS2 is too strong and the detector is saturated, which induces the disappear of peak head.

(a)–(b), 3D AFM topographic images of a WSe2 island and islands inside triangle holes, respectively; (c), Raman spectrum collected from the pillar in (a); (d) schematic of a growing WSe2 flake meeting a GQD cluster; (e) schematic of the growth of WSe2 holes in early state; (f) schematic of growth of WSe2 holes in final state.

head.ce:title: Deposition of porous few-layer WSe2 flakes with high density of exposed edge sites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, 310018, Hangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electronics and Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, 310018, Hangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Changshu Institute of Technology, 215500, Changshu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, remarkably differing to reported well-defined triangular WSe2 flakes, porous WSe2 flakes with large amount of holes were deposited using graphene quantum dots(GQDs) as promoters. By employing this method, triangular or circular thorough holes can be efficiently deposited on basal plane of WSe2 flakes. The size of holes and density of edges vary with layer number of WSe2 basal plane. Highest density of edges emerges on 4-layer WSe2 flake, which is greatly enhanced by 580% compared to those well-defined triangular 4-layer flakes. Finally we propose that the growth of holes is validated by poor wetting between WSe2 and GQDs, while the final shape is determined by crystal symmetry, diffusion and thermal dynamics.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, as an important member of transition metal dichalcogenides(TMDs), WSe2 has drew much attention owing to its ultra low thermal conductivity(0.05 W m−1K−1) [1] and high carrier mobility(>100 cm2 V-1S−1) [2]. WSe2 possesses periodic layered structure, in which a layer consists of a plane of tungsten atoms sandwiched between two planes of selenide atoms [3]. When the thickness of WSe2 crystal is thinned from bulk to monolayer, its energy band transists from indirect (band gap∼1.2eV) to direct band (band band gap ∼1.7eV) [3]. High exciton binding energy of monolayer WSe2 (∼790 meV) enables observable excitonic luminescence even at room temperature [4]. These remarkable characteristics indicate potential applications of WSe2 in light-emitting devices [5,6], thermoelectronics [7–9], optoelectronics [10,11] and efficient catalysts in hydrogen generation(HG) through water-splitting [1,12,13].

Edges are critical to electronic [14,15] and magnetic properties [16,17] of WSe2. Zigzag-edge of WSe2 is expected to be metallic and magnetic, while armchair-edge is expected to be semiconductive and non-magnetic [14,18]. Moreover, the HG performance of WSe2 strongly relies on the density of exposed edge sites because only edge is chemically active while basal plane is inert [19–21]. The applications of WSe2 in HG and future magnetic devices emphasize the importance of preparing high quality WSe2 samples with high density of exposed edges. If the total length of exposed edge in an unit area, i.e. density of edges as defined in this study, can be enhanced by creating more edges, much higher efficiency of HG is expected to be obtained [22,23]. In despite of intensive works on deposition of MoS2 with high density of exposed edges [22,23], to our best knowledge, the studies of depositing high quality WSe2 samples with high density of edges are quite rare. In early reports, well-defined monolayer or thick layer WSe2 flakes with triangular or polygonal shape had been deposited by chemical vapor deposition(CVD) [10,11] or vapor transportation(VT) [24,25]. However, the obtained well-defined WSe2 flakes only present low density of edges. In other reports, Zou et al. [13] deposited three-dimensional(3D) WSe2 nano flowers on carbon fiber via CVD process, while Wang et al. [12] used a solvothermal routine to deposit WSe2 on graphene and reduced graphene oxide flakes. In these works, the density of edges had not been obviously enhanced. These initial progresses indicate novel methods to prepare high quality WSe2 with enhanced density of edges are still desirable.

In this study, porous WSe2 few-layer flakes had been deposited by employing VT method and GQDs as promoters. Remarkably different to commonly prepared well-defined triangular WSe2 flakes [10,11,24,25], our porous flakes exhibit large amount of thorough holes with triangular or circular shape. The density of edges is greatly enhanced up to 580% compared to well-defined flakes without holes. The possible growth mechanism is briefly discussed. In a comparative study of CVD-growth of MoS2 using GQDs as promoter, in contrast to WSe2, good surface wetting enables the growth of large-scale monolayer and thick-layer of MoS2 without holes.

In conventional VT growth, WSe2 firstly sublimates into vapor phase at high temperature (typically 950 °C), subsequently WSe2 vapor is transported to a substrate placed at low temperature(∼850 °C, Fig. 1 a) and finally nucleates into WSe2 crystal [24]. In this study, high purity(99.5%) WSe2 powder was placed at the center of a quartz tube furnace(Fig. 1a). In the first step, in order to deposit well-defined triangular WSe2 flakes, cleaned SiO2(thickness 300 nm)/Si substrates were placed ∼9 cm away (downstream) from the source (Fig. 1a). Before ramping the temperature, the tube furnace was pumped to a vacuum <0.01 Torr. Ar2/H2(volume ratio = 19/1) gas with flow rate of 15sccm was introduced as carrier gas. The furnace temperature was ramped to 950°Cat a rate of 20 °C/min(Fig. 1b). The vacuum was kept at ∼20 Torr during growth. After growth finalized, the furnace was cooled down to room temperature quickly under flowing carrier gas. In the second step, water solutions of GQDs were spin-coated on cleaned SiO2(thickness 300 nm)/Si substrates in order to deposit porous WSe2 flakes. The growth time was 40min. Other growth conditions are identical to those used for deposition of well-defined flakes.

Monolayer and few-layer MoS2 flakes were deposited by using a CVD routine reported in our previous work [26]. Briefly, solid MoO3(2 mg) and S (100 mg) powders were placed in heating Zone-1 and Zone-2 of a quartz tube(100 mm diameter), respectively. SiO2(300 nm)/silicon substrates were placed face-down on a quartz boat. Argon with flow rate of 80sccm was used as carrier gas. The temperature of Zone-2 was ramped to 680 °C with a rate of 20 °C/min and kept for 20 mins, then cooled down to room temperature naturally. The temperature in Zone-2 was not deliberately controlled.

Optical images of WSe2 and MoS2 were obtained using an optical microscope. Topographic images of WSe2 and MoS2 flakes were measured using an atomic force microscope(AFM, Park). Raman and PL spectra of WSe2 and MoS2 samples were measured using a Raman spectrometer(Renishaw invia) with excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm.

Optical images of monolayer, few-layer and thick-layer WSe2 flakes deposited without aid of GQDs are presented in Fig. 1c, Fig. S1 and Fig. 1d–e, respectively. The morphology of WSe2 strongly depends on distance from substrate to source(D). Well-defined triangular flakes only grow in a certain area, i.e. D = 9 cm and a width of ∼1 cm (Fig. S1a and Figs. S1b–d). If the substrate is too close to the source(D < 8 cm), WSe2 flakes or islands are hardly observed. Whereas if the substrate is too far from source(D > 10 cm), WSe2 tends to nucleate into small(several hundreds of nanometers) and thick islands (Figs. S1e–f). The thickness of well-defined triangular flakes can be controlled by varying growth time and flow rate of carrier gas. At constant flow rate of 15sccm, monolayer WSe2 samples can be obtained with growth time of 25mins(Fig. 1c). While with longer growth time(>30min), thick-layer flakes are obtained (Fig. 1d–e). It can be seen these few-layer and thick-layer flakes exhibit well-defined triangles with lateral lengths of 10–20μm. It's well know that such triangle shape is induced by crystal symmetry and growth conditions [27–29]. The shape of triangular WSe2 flakes are determined by two types of terminating edges, i.e. W-edges and Se-edges. According to the crystal symmetry(
P 6
m 3
/ m m c
for bulk and
P
6 ¯
m 2
for monolayer) [3], these two edges can be (
1 ¯
010
) Se-edge or (
10
1 ¯
0
) W-edge, respectively, with angle of 60° [30]. In ideal case, a hexagonal morphology of WSe2 flakes with three (
1 ¯
010
) Se-edges and three (
10
1 ¯
0
) W-edge interspersed is expected [30]. However, in the crystal growth progress, two types of edges usually have different growth rate. If growth rate of a type of edge is faster than another one, triangular shape flake is expected to emerge [27–29]. AFM topographic images of monolayer and few-layer flakes are presented in Fig. 1f and g, respectively. Their corresponding height profiles across edges are presented in Fig. 1h. The height of monolayer is calculated to be 0.7 nm, which agrees well with the early reports [31]. The height of the few-layer flake in Fig. 1g is calculated to be 2.0 nm, which indicates this few-layer flake has three layers [31].

Scratches are typically inhomogeneous nucleation sites, in which defect reduces the nucleation free energy and makes nucleation and growth to be much easier [32]. Optical images of WSe2 flakes(F1, F2 and F3) deposited neighboring scratches are shown in Fig. 2 a-c, respectively(Please see Fig. S2a for large-scale image). The optical contrasts of these flakes vary from deep purple(F1) to blue(F2) and finally into deep green(F3), which indicates increasing thickness of flakes. Surprisingly a large amount of holes exist on the basal plane of these flakes. It can be seen most of holes in flakes F1-F3 have regular triangular shape. Other large polygonal holes are actually merged by two or more small triangular holes. Line profiles across the holes of F1-F3(at marked positions of Fig. 2a–c) are plotted in Fig. S2b. It can be seen the hole areas have the same height as the substrate. Moreover, the areas inside these holes exhibit optical contrast identical to the substrate. Combining the optical contrast and AFM height, it can be concluded that these holes are thorough holes instead of pits. It's also worth to mention that these triangular holes are arranged upside down, while the basal plane of WSe2 points to nearly horizontal direction. The lateral sides of holes rotate for a certain angle compared to that of WSe2 basal plane edge. After Careful calculation, this angle is determined to be 60±5°. If the edges of holes are identical to those of edges of WSe2 basal flake, the edge of holes should be parallel to the flake edge, which is in contrary to Fig. 2. This also indicates the edges of holes are different to that of basal WSe2 flakes. However, due to instrumental limitations, we are not able to distinguish whether the edges are W-edges or Se edges.

AFM topographic images of flakes F1-F3 are shown in Fig. 2d–f, respectively. Height profiles across the edges (along dashed lines) of F1-F3 are shown as insets in Fig. 2d–f, respectively. By using these profiles, the basal plane thickness of flakes F1-F3 can be determined to be 2.2 nm, 2.9 nm and 3.6 nm, respectively. Therefore, their layer numbers are estimated to be 3 layers, 4 layers and 5–6 layers [31] by using monolayer thickness of ∼0.7 nm calculated in Fig. 1h. In Fig. 2a–f, it can be found the size of holes decreases apparently with increasing flake thickness. In flake F1, the lateral length of the holes is 3–6 μm. In flake F2, the lateral length of most of holes falls in 2–3 μm. While in flake F3, the lateral length of the holes are reduced to be 0.5–2 μm. The possible mechanism for such decreasing size will be discussed in following section.

In order to easily quantify density of edges of porous WSe2 flakes, we defined an edge density ratio as R
L = L
t /L
l , where L
t is summarized lateral length of hole edges and flake edges of a flake(total length of black solid lines of inset in Fig. 2g), L
l is the total geometric lateral length of a triangle enveloping this flake(total length of green dashed lines of inset in Fig. 2g). Calculated R
L of different flakes are shown in Fig. 2g. In well-defined triangular flake without holes(annotated as F0, corresponding to the flakes in Fig. 1d), L
t = L
l and R
L = 1. For 4-layer flake F2, R
L is greatly enhanced to be 5.8, which indicates its density of edges is greatly enhanced by 580% compared to well-defined 4-layer triangular flakes. For 3-layer flake F1 and thick-layer flake F3, the density of edges are enhanced by 308% and 280%, respectively. We also calculated the area ratio of holes R
A = A
m
/A, where A
m and A are the summarized area of holes and the area of the whole flake, respectively. The calculated results are shown in Fig. 2g. As no holes in well-defined triangular flake F0, its R
A is 0. Again, R
A of 4-layer flake is as high as 38%. Either decreasing (3-layer)or increasing layer numbers(5–6 layer) is going to reduce R
A . Calculated R
L and R
A in Fig. 2g indicate GQDs are more efficient to produce highest density of edges on 4-layer flake.

Raman spectra collected on basal plane of flakes F1-F3 are presented in Fig. 2h. These three flakes exhibit analogy Raman bands, i.e. a prominent double-peak band at ∼250 cm−1 and three weak peaks in 350–400 cm−1. This double-peak band can be further assigned to peaks at 249 cm−1(A’+E′ mode) and 258 cm−1 (2LA mode) [33]. Raman peaks at 359, 373 and 393 cm−1 can be assigned to high order modes [33]. Such Raman features agree well with the reported Raman spectra of high quality well-defined triangular WSe2 flakes [31], indicating high crystalline quality of our porous WSe2 flakes [33].

Blank SiO2 surface represents homogeneous nucleation sites. At these sites no impurities or other defects can be used to reduce the free energy, the nucleation and growth is relatively difficult [32]. In contrast to WSe2 flakes growing via inhomogeneous mode, the porous WSe2 flakes growing via homogeneous mode have different morphologies. Fig. 3 a–d presents optical images of four WSe2 flakes at progressively growing states. In Fig. 3a–d, blue color spots are GQD clusters. The AFM topographic images corresponding to Fig. 3a–d are shown in Fig. 3e–h. Judging by optical contrast and flake thickness of 2 nm using height profile across the edge(Fig. 3e), the basal planes of flakes in Fig. 3a and b can be determined to be 3-layer. In Fig. 3a–b half holes with circular shape are occasionally found on the edges. In Fig. 3b–d and Fig. 3f–h(inset of Fig. 3f), only small circular holes instead of triangular holes can be found. The diameter of these holes varies from 100 to 500 nm. It's worth to mention that as shown by Fig. 3a–h and Fig. S3, neither dendritic nor triangular WSe2 flakes grows around GQD. This indicates WSe2 has bad wetting on the surface of GQD clusters.

In order to verify the critical influence of surface wetting to the growth of holes on atomic thickness layers, a comparative CVD-growth experiment was carried out to deposit MoS2 flakes with GQDs as promoters. Optical images of prepared MoS2 flakes are shown in Fig. 4 a–c. AFM topographic images of corresponding marked areas are shown in Fig. 4d–f, respectively. It can be seen in Fig. 4a and d that MoS2 flakes nucleate around a GQD cluster and finally grow into big starfish-like flakes. The separation between two prominent Raman peaks (A
1g , out of plane mode;
E
2 g
1
, in plane mode) [34] are calculated to be 20 cm−1 for these starfish-like flakes(Fig. 4g), which indicates their monolayer nature [35]. The height(∼1 nm) calculated from the AFM height profile(inset of Fig. 4d) also indicates these starfish-like flakes are monolayer. The monolayer nature is verified by their strong PL band at 670 nm(Fig. 4h). In Fig. 4b, c and 4e, f, many dendrite flakes grow around GQD clusters. Large peak separation(26 cm−1, Fig. 4g) and weak PL intensity(Fig. 4h) indicates these dendrite flakes are thick-layers. It can be seen (Fig. 4a–c) that both monolayer and thick layer MoS2 flakes grow around GQDs clusters, which indicating good wetting between MoS2 and GQDs. However, in contrast to WSe2, no round or triangular holes can be found either on the starfish-like monolayer or dendrite thick-layer flakes. Such remarkable different influence of GQDs on the growth of MoS2 and WSe2 could be attributed to different wetting ability. Good wetting between MoS2 and GQDs cluster provides more nucleation sites of MoS2 on the surface of GQDs cluster, which finally benefits the growth of starfish-like or dendrite flakes. This agrees well with the previous reports using graphene as promoter to deposit MoS2 [36], indicating the critical role of surface wetting in the growth of porous flakes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of holes on porous flakes
ce:section.ce:para: Three dimensional(3D) AFM topographic images of two typical triangular holes were measured and presented in Fig. 5 a and b, respectively. In Fig. 5a, a tall island with height as ∼140 nm and full width at half maximum(FWHM) as ∼500 nm is observed in the middle of a triangular hole. While in Fig. 5b, a handful of small islands with height of 10–15 nm appear. A Raman spectrum collected from the tall island in Fig. 5a is shown in Fig. 5c. Dominant Raman peaks at 249 cm−1, 259 cm−1 and three high order Raman peaks at 350-400 cm−1 analog to Raman spectra in Fig. 2h, indicating the presence of WSe2 on this island [33]. More interestingly, two broad peaks at 1341 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 can be simultaneously observed. The shape and position of these two peaks analog to those of graphene quantum dots [37]. Therefore, peak at 1341 cm−1 can be assigned to D mode, while peak at 1578 cm−1 can be assigned to G mode [37]. These features indicates the tall island in Fig. 5a is composed by WSe2 and GQDs.

Prior to further analysis, it's necessary to discuss the influence of GQD clusters on the growth of WSe2 nanocrystals. An AFM topographic image of GQD clusters on SiO2/Si substrate is shown in Fig. S4a. In this image doughnut islands with diameter of ∼800 nm and height ∼20 nm are GQD clusters, while tiny white spots are WSe2 nanocrystals. Four doughnut clusters are marked as T1-T4. Height profiles along marked lines across these four clusters are shown in Fig. S4b. The centre of T1 is clear, indicating absence of nanocrystals. While inside T2-T4, WSe2 nanocrystals with different height can be seen. In T2, a small and short nanocrystal emerges, indicating its early growing state. In T3, a nanocrystal grows up with height of 20 nm. In T4, a tall pillar-like island with height of ∼100 nm is observed. Such increasing height of WSe2 crystals reflects their progressing growth states inside GQD clusters. As shown by Fig. S3, WSe2 has bad wetting on the surface of GQD cluster. Possibly due to this factor, if a WSe2 nanocrystal nucleates inside a GQD doughnut cluster, it cannot grow on the surface of GQD. Therefore, this WSe2 nanocrystal cannot grow in horizontal direction, but only grow up in vertical direction and finally become a pillar-like island. This process can be briefly summarized as that bad wetting and the doughnut shape of GQD confine the growth of WSe2 nanocrystal. Combining confinement induced growth of WSe2 nanocrystals inside GQD clusters of Fig. S4, it's reasonable to suppose a double layer model for the pillar-like island in Fig. 5a: doughnut GQD clusters at the bottom(Fig. 5e) and a WSe2 pillar-like island on the top(Fig. 5e).

The formation of WSe2 holes is validated by competitive growth between the WSe2 island and the flake around the doughnut cluster. The confinement induced growth pillar-like WSe2 of Fig. 5a analogs to that of T4 of Fig. S4. Whilst the growth of this pillar-like WSe2 crystal in vertical direction, the WSe2 flake around the doughnut cluster also simultaneously grows in horizontal direction. At constant mass flow rate of WSe2, there exists a competitive growth. Tall height of the WSe2 pillar-like crystal and large size of holes indicate much faster growth of pillar-like crystal than triangular flake, and also fast diffusion of WSe2 from triangular flake to pillar-like crystal. In another word, the pillar-like WSe2 nanocrystal grows up by eating up the WSe2 flake around this GQD cluster(shown by Fig. 5e). The continuing diffusion of WSe2 from the nearby WSe2 flake finally leaves a hole around this GQD cluster. It is interesting that only the islands directly within WSe2 flake or nearby can grow up, while the islands far away from WSe2 flake are extremely short (Fig. 2d–f). This indicates the diffusion of WSe2 mass from tube flow only has negligible influence to the growth of WSe2 islands.

The growth and final shape of holes are theoretically affected by three main factors: (Ⅰ) crystal lattice symmetry; (Ⅱ) thermal dynamics; and (Ⅲ) WSe2 mass diffusion [32]. Yamamoto et al. indicate the ideal shape of etching pits on MoS2 is determined by the lattice symmetry [38]. This analysis can also be adapted to interpret the ideal shape of WSe2 holes. The edges of triangular hole are either
(
1 ¯
010 )
Se-edges and
( 10
1 ¯
0 )
W-edges interspersed. Because these two edges are intersected with an angle of 60°, a hole with regular triangular shape is finally expected to emerge. The crystal symmetry only determines the ideal shape of holes. The growth of ideal shape needs Se and W atoms having enough energy and speed to migrate into equilibrium positions [38]. As a universal factor, high growth temperature is desirable to fulfilling these premises [32]. However, as we pointed out in section 3.1 and section Fig. 1a, in VT growth of WSe2 flakes, too high or too low temperature would stop the growth of WSe2 flakes. This also indicates the triangle holes only grow in a very narrow area on the substrate with suitable temperature.

Thermal dynamics is also an important factor that affects the growth of holes. The total free energy variation in growth of a hole can be expressed as
G T
=− Δ
G v
+ S
σ s
+ L
σ L
, where
Δ
G v
is volume free energy change, S refers to surface area of the pillar grain, and L is total edge length of a hole;
σ s
and
σ L
are surface free energy and edge free energy, respectively [32].
σ L
strongly depends on the lattice symmetry.
(
1 ¯
010 )
Se-edges and
( 10
1 ¯
0 )
W-edges have lowest free energy [38], which indicates triangle would be an equilibrium hole shape. It should be noted that the increasing hole size and edge length shall raise the free energy, indicating hole would not grow without the participation of pillar grain. The growth of the pillar grain inside a hole causes a large
Δ
G v
, which validates the growth of holes. Low temperature in the temperature range of triangular flake growth would favor the nucleation of pillar grain.

WSe2 mass diffusion is the another factor affecting the hole shape. The diffusion of WSe2 from hole edge to pillar grain affects the size of hole, while the diffusion along hole edge reconstruct the hole shape. According to Fick's first diffusion law
J =− D
∂ C
∂ x
, the diffusive flux J should be proportional to diffusion coefficient D and mass gradient
∂ C
∂ x
[32]. The diffusion coefficient strongly depends on growth temperature. High growth temperature induces large D and fast diffusion. As the growth of WSe2 is limited in a small growth temperature range, D is also limited. The mass gradient strongly depends on the lay numbers and WSe2 distribution in the tube. In hole on flakes with larger layer numbers has high mass gradient. However, the distribution of WSe2 in the tube is quite complicate, which also complicates the whole mass gradient.

As discussed above, fulfilling crystal symmetry and fast diffusion needs higher temperature, but fulfilling thermal dynamics needs lower temperature. This indicates the holes can only grow in a certain temperature range and also certain locations on the substrate, which agrees with our experiment. Further interpreting the circular or triangular shape of holes needs to consider crystal symmetry, diffusion and thermal dynamics in all. Holes with large size have long total length of edges. In this case the term of edge free energy is dominant. For large holes, triangle shape fulfilling the crystal symmetry requirement has the lowest edge free energy and total free energy [38]. In order to reduce the total free energy, WSe2 on the edges of holes diffuses and finally reconstructs into a triangle hole, schematically shown in Fig. 5f. However, with decreasing size of holes, the total edge length of a hole greatly decrease. In the total free energy equation the term of edge free energy becomes not dominant. In this case in fast diffusion region, small holes still can become triangular(inset of Fig. S5). In contrary, in slow diffusion region, circular shape, as a nonequilibrium state, can be preserved (observed in Fig. 3a–d and Fig. S5).

If there are multiple nanocrystals inside the hole area, each nanocrystal tends to grow into a pillar-like island. However, due to limited total WSe2 mass in a certain volume, the height of these islands is expected to be much lower than that of a single island. This case corresponds to the multiple islands observed in Fig. 5b. It should be noted that the decreasing hole size with increasing layer numbers observed in Fig. 2a–c can also be correlated to this diffusion mechanism. Growing of a WSe2 pillar-like island with specific height and cross-section on different layers needs constant volume of WSe2. Thick-layer has more WSe2 than thin layer in an unit area. Consequently on thicker layer, the area consumed by a pillar-like island should be less than that on thinner layer, resulting into a hole with smaller area in thick-layer.

In summary, by using GQDs as promoters, porous WSe2 flakes with high density of triangular or circular holes were prepared by VT method. The size of holes, varying from several hundreds of nanometers to several microns, and density of edges strongly depend on layer numbers of WSe2 basal plane. Highest density of edges, which is greatly enhanced by 580%, is observed on four-layer flakes, which also induces total hole area 38% of the whole flake. However, in CVD-growth of MoS2 flakes by using GQDs as promoters, no holes had been found either on CVD-grown monolayer or few-layers. We propose that crystal symmetry, diffusion and thermal dynamics are three major factors that determine the shape of holes.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.vacuum.2017.08.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-like WSe2 nanosheets for efficient and stable hydrogen evolution
